items.q: What is the magnitude of the vector (3, 4)?
items.misconception: The magnitude is 7 — you add the components.
items.random_wrong: The magnitude is 12 — you multiply the components.
items.why: Students don't connect vector magnitude with Pythagoras; they treat vectors as ordered pairs to add or multiply directly.

items.q: If the dot product of two non-zero vectors is zero, what does that mean?
items.correct: The vectors are perpendicular to each other.
items.misconception: It means at least one of the vectors is the zero vector.
items.random_wrong: It means the vectors are parallel.
items.why: Students assume 'zero result' implies 'zero input', missing the geometric meaning of the dot product.

items.misconception: 8xy, by combining all terms regardless of variable.
items.random_wrong: 9xy, by adding all the numbers and merging the letters.
items.why: Students treat different variables as interchangeable, missing that only terms with the same variable (and power) can be combined.
The answer examiners want is: 5. “25” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's (0.6, 0.8). “(3, 4)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
